name: Yuki
message: "Hey, Tomosane! Your tie's crooked!"

name: Tomosane
message: "It's fine. I'm already running late!"

name: Yuki
message: "It is not fine! Here, turn towards me."
"As she said that, Yuki began fixing my tie."

name: Yuki
message: "Do you think Hasaki-chan's having fun at university?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Dunno. I don't really know what it's like at university, so I can't say for sure."

name: Yuki
message: "Jeez. If it were my personality in control, I could have gotten us in with a scholarship, no problem."

name: Tomosane
message: "Like I care. Besides, how could you even study?"

name: Yuki
message: "Hm? Well, because I'm intelligent of course."

name: Tomosane
message: "You use the same brain as me, though..."

name: Yuki
message: "Sure, but our consciousnesses are different, and we use different neural circuits.
We might be sharing the same brain, but I use it in a different way than you do."

name: Yuki
message: "It could be the same mobile suit, but when the Major pilots it, it's three times faster, right?
Basically, I'm the Major, and you're Slender."

name: Tomosane
message: "Who the hell is Slender?"

name: Yuki
message: "Sergeant Slender, of course!
He trusted in his major's words that 'It doesn't matter what happens, as long as you don't get hit,' and supported him, but in the end he got taken out in one shot!"

name: Yuki
message: "After he got hit, the Major was all shocked like, 'So its rifle is as powerful as a battleship's main cannon...' But how awful of the Major! How is Slender supposed to rest in peace after dying in such a pitiful way!? Jeez! What the hell is up with that, Tomosane!?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Wh-- What are you getting mad at me for...?"

name: Yuki
message: "Hehe, just kidding. Anyway. You know, Tomosane, fixing your tie like this, I just thought that it's almost like we're newlyweds. I just wanted to talk a little longer."

name: Tomosane
message: "Are you *trying* to make me late!?"

name: Yuki
message: "That's not really my intention. Hey, standing here like this, it really feels like you've gotten taller."

name: Tomosane
message: "No, I'm pretty sure I'm fully grown..."

name: Yuki
message: "That can't be right. You've definitely grown since then. You're slowly growing into an adult... I think. Ah, look! You're even growing a beard!"
Saying that, Yuki tugged down on my tie.

name: Tomosane
message: "Ghhk!!"

name: Tomosane
message: "What the hell are you doing? If you pull down like that, you're gonna wring my neck!"

name: Yuki
message: "Hmm let's see... just a little～."
Yuki began rubbing her cheek against my jaw.

name: Yuki
message: "Hmm? Your cheek's a little fuzzy. Did you shave properly～?"

name: Tomosane
message: "It's fine. You can barely tell just by looking at my face anyway."

name: Yuki
message: "You can't be making excuses like that. Your Onee-san won't allow it."

name: Tomosane
message: "Right now the only thing I can't be is late!"

name: Yuki
message: "Oh you'll be fine. If you run out the door at full-speed, another three minutes should be fine."

name: Tomosane
message: "Why the hell should I have to sprint all the way to the station first thing in the morning!?"

name: Yuki
message: "Well, how should I put it? So I can do this～."
She rubbed her cheek against my chin again.

name: Yuki
message: "This way I can really feel how your beard's growing."

name: Tomosane
message: "You don't need to feel that!"

name: Yuki
message: "Yes I do! It's super important!
Physical intimacy in the mornings is essential to a married couple's lifestyle!"

name: Tomosane
message: "But we aren't married..."

name: Yuki
message: "Well, that's true... Someday you will fall in love with someone, and that's when I'll rest in peace with no regrets. But until then, we're a couple!"

name: Yuki
message: "Hehe, so fuzzy..."

name: Tomosane
message: "A couple until then...? Look, I'm really gonna be late!"

name: Yuki
message: "Another minute and a half should be fine! Believe in the Tomosane that I believe in!"

name: Tomosane
message: "Then let me shave!"

name: Yuki
message: "You're the one that said it was fine to leave it!"

name: Tomosane
message: "If you're just gonna do stupid crap like this, then I'll shave it off!"
I pushed Yuki off of me and ran into the bathroom.
We carried on like that, and this ghost with the black, frilly dress stayed with me.
Whether she's really a ghost or just a figment of my imagination, it made no difference to me.
I was the only one who could see her, and she was always by my side.
Yuki spoke as she stared at the sky from the train's window.

name: Yuki
message: "Pioneers of aerodynamics."

name: Tomosane
message: "What's that?"

name: Yuki
message: "Alexandre Gustave Eiffel, he stands in front of the tower that he built by himself."

name: Yuki
message: "If that's the case, don't you think that a girl would at least want to stand next to someone she loves?"

name: Tomosane
message: "I have absolutely no idea what you're talking about."

name: Yuki
message: "I'm talking about the dream of the Aerodynamic Girl."
A lot's happened since then.
After graduation, I started working.
Considering everything that happened in that incident, it's a miracle that I was able to find a job.
A patron of the bar happened to be an executive director of a certain company. He took a liking to me and hired me.
Having come to a bar like that, the guy was probably gay, but he never hit on me or harassed me.
Rather, it felt like he was genuinely concerned about me.
I had considered post-secondary education, but didn't really want to do any more studying, so it's for the best that I ended up working for his company instead.

name: Yuki
message: "You're always lying to yourself like that..."

name: Tomosane
message: "How can you tell what I'm thinking?"

name: Yuki
message: "Probably because we've been together for so long..."

name: Yuki
message: "When you feel strongly about something, or if you're thinking something over, I can just tell... Just now, you were thinking something you've thought over and over, trying to convince yourself."

name: Yuki
message: "You were saying it to the you of the present."

name: Yuki
message: "You're such a sweetheart..."
As she said that, Yuki brought her face close to mine.

name: Tomosane
message: "Don't get so close..."

name: Yuki
message: "What, don't you like having such a nice view of my breasts from this position?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Not really."

name: Yuki
message: "Is that so?"
I looked away, and Yuki spoke.

name: Yuki
message: "You're working for Hasaki-chan's sake, right Tomosane?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Of course not. I knew she'd be able to get her scholarship. There's no reason for me to be working my ass off over that."

name: Yuki
message: "What do you mean? When you took this job you still didn't know whether or not she'd get it."
She really was a pain in the ass.

name: Yuki
message: "Well either way, Hasaki-chan's been attending that crazy "Department of Informatics" place, yeah?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Yeah, that's right. What do you think she's planning to do?"

name: Yuki
message: "She was reading a book on BMIs."

name: Tomosane
message: "Bee Em Eyes? What's that?"

name: Yuki
message: "It's an acronym for 'Brain Machine Interface,' BMI.
Basically it was about research into connecting machines to the human brain."

name: Tomosane
message: "Research into connecting machines to the brain? Why was she reading something like that?"

name: Yuki
message: "It sounds like she plans to attend a seminar held by one of the leading researchers in the field. Maybe she wants to work on developing those kinds of machines in the future?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Why would she?"

name: Yuki
message: "Maybe she feels like I've been monopolizing you, and she wants to force herself between us?
She'd jump into your brain to stop me like, 'I won't let you have Tomo Nii-san all to yourself!!’

name: Yuki
message: "Hasaki-chan can see me, but she can't exactly physically interact with me, right?
But if she could use a machine to align your brains, don't you think maybe she could?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Is there really a way to connect two brains with a machine?"

name: Yuki
message: "It is theoretically, from the viewpoint of the Mind/Brain Identity Theory."

name: Tomosane
message: "What's that?"

name: Yuki
message: "Essentially, it's the idea that the physical brain's workings are what generates the 'mind.’ That 'consciousness' is born simply from neurons firing."

name: Tomosane
message: "Isn't that just common sense? The brain is what powers the mind, right? I don't think many people would argue with the idea that your consciousness comes from neuron activation."

name: Yuki
message: "True, that would be the common sense view of it."

name: Yuki
message: "But even so, there's a deep-rooted criticism of this way of thinking.
There's a lot of resistance to the idea that you can reduce our minds and consciences entirely to physical explanations."

name: Yuki
message: "'Consciousness is nothing more than the result a physical reaction.’
Put that way, it's a bit sad, don't you think?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Really? I thought that's all there was to it..."

name: Yuki
message: "For example, let's say we want to pinpoint the feeling of 'pain' in a physicalistic sense. To do that, it would be a simple matter of finding which nerve fibers fire signals when 'pain' is felt in the body, and then it would be possible to explain the phenomenon of 'pain,', you would think."

name: Yuki
message: "If you could find the common nerve fibers that activate in everyone's brain when they feel 'pain,' to generalize it, then that would be 'pain's' true form."

name: Tomosane
message: "Makes sense to me."

name: Yuki
message: "But you know, if you think about it for a minute, isn't this kind of strange?
For example: What if we removed this bundle of 'pain' producing nerve fibers from the rest of the brain?"

name: Yuki
message: "We could stick it in a test tube or something with some cultivation fluid to keep it alive."

name: Yuki
message: "The nerve fibers would still be able to fire off signals while floating in the fluid."

name: Yuki
message: "These signals that are supposedly the source of pain, but this bundle of nerve fibers in a test tube wouldn't actually be feeling any pain in this case, right?"

name: Yuki
message: "Nobody would be feeling pain at all.
It's just a bundle of nerve fibers sending signals in a test tube."

name: Yuki
message: "Isn't it weird? That's what I'm getting at."

name: Tomosane
message: "Well you'd need other parts of the brain to actually process and feel the pain, right?
If you just remove the part that sends the pain signals without the parts that receive them, it's a bit pointless, isn't it?"

name: Yuki
message: "Sure, but if you need other parts of the brain, you can't really say that bundle of nerve fibers is the 'true form' of pain. That'd be putting the cart before the horse, right?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Then I'm not sure... I don't know."

name: Yuki
message: "Hehe, the genius Yuki has splendidly bested Tomosane in this debate!"

name: Tomosane
message: "Were we having a debate?"

name: Yuki
message: "Your Yuki-nee is always ready for a battle of wits!"

name: Tomosane
message: "Then, let's keep talking for a little while."

name: Tomosane
message: "This might be a rough comparison,
but it's kind of like having a game console powered on that's not connected to a TV or a controller."

name: Tomosane
message: "'The game's running, but you can't play it.' Therefore, 'The game can't actually do anything.' Is that basically what you're saying?"

name: Yuki
message: "Not a great metaphor, but I get what you're trying to say."

name: Tomosane
message: "Something as complicated as 'Consciousness' would require a certain fixed amount of information, wouldn't it? I think that having more than that fixed amount of information is what gives meaning to the variety and integrity of the brain's signals."

name: Tomosane
message: "'Consciousness' is born from the diversity of signals fired in unison by nerve bundles. So if we separate those bundles from the brain to observe them, the brain itself loses its original function."

name: Tomosane
message: "If a part of the brain is damaged, it can lead to changes in personality and other disorders. For example, damage to the cerebrum can make it difficult to keep track of time."

name: Tomosane
message: "The doctor might ask you 'What season is it right now?' and you'd respond 'Summer.' 'But, aren't you wearing a sweater right now? Why are you wearing a sweater in the middle of summer?' he'd ask, and you'd have no idea. You'd be unable to match temperature with the seasons."

name: Tomosane
message: "You'd think it was 'morning' every time you woke up. Even if you took a ten-minute nap in the afternoon, once you woke up you'd think it was 'morning' again."

name: Tomosane
message: "You might take nap after filling up on lunch, and wake up under an hour later.
Physically you'd still be full, but because you'd think it's morning, your brain would think 'I'm hungry,' and you'd think it was time for breakfast."

name: Tomosane
message: "There's no end to examples like this."

name: Tomosane
message: "The brain's an extremely complex and intricately defined structure.
We probably won't fully map it out for a long time."

name: Yuki
message: "Oh, you mean like how at the Howard Hughes Medical Institute, they did that experiment on a fruit fly where they made a 50-nanometer slice into its brain and have been trying to create a map out of its neurons?"

name: Yuki
message: "I have access to most of the same knowledge you do, you know.
They predicted that even if it was scanned at a rate of 1 million gigabytes per day, it would take 20 years to fully map out."

name: Yuki
message: "They also said that there would be so much image data of just the fruit fly's brain that it would fill an entire warehouse full of HDDs."

name: Yuki
message: "If they did this with a human brain, they estimated that it would take at least a hundred years! This never-ending project is where the whole field of neuroscience comes from. You could say it's the modern-day Yokohama station!"

name: Tomosane
message: "Are you retarded!? Normally you'd compare it to the Sagrada Familia or something!"
Hearing such a stupid comparison made me suddenly shout out loud.
The train was packed with people on their way to work and school, and I caught more than a few stares for my outburst.

name: Yuki
message: "Don't shout like that! People are gonna think you're crazy."
W-Well, to be frank, most people would say that someone who has conversations with a voice in his head is pretty crazy."

name: Yuki
message: "I resent that remark."

name: Tomosane
message: "Oh, shut up..."

name: Yuki
message: "'Where is my mind!'
I've gone insane! Something like that?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Well, you might not be too far off..."

name: Yuki
message: "'Where is my mind?'
Where is my consciousness?
If you put it like that, it's pretty close to what we're talking about..."

name: Tomosane
message: "Close enough for now. Anyway, they say the brain has 100 billion neurons and 500 trillion synapses. When you consider how many possible combinations of signals there are among them, there's an unfathomable amount of possibilities."

name: Yuki
message: "That's true. And there's more.
If you were to create a simulation model of the human neocortex and sensory organs and all the connections between them, you'd need something as powerful as a supercomputer the size of a city block."

name: Yuki
message: "To power such a machine, you'd need an entire dedicated 1000 Megawatt nuclear power plant.
Something with such an absurd amount of power would generate an extreme amount of heat, so you would need to draw an entire man-made river to keep it cool."

name: Yuki
message: "Such a massive piece of information technology equipment can fit in something as small as our craniums! It's amazing! A mystery of the human body! Surely, humanity must be God's creation!"

name: Tomosane
message: "But with further developments in neuromorphic chips that simulate the firing conditions of human neurons, that much heat won't be necessary, right?"

name: Yuki
message: "You mean those things that physically mimic neurons and synapses? Those are still an unknown variable, it's not clear if they'll ever be viable. Many professionals remain highly skeptical."

name: Yuki
message: "For starters, the length and density of neurons is extremely precise."

name: Yuki
message: "It's said that if the neurons become too thin, their action potential will become unstable. If they become too dense, they'll die due to thermal runaway, and if they're too big, the distance between the nerve fiber bundles will increase too much, slowing down the exchange of information and ultimately the processing of the brain itself."

name: Yuki
message: "Could humans really build something so precise as a brain circuit into a silicon wafer?"

name: Tomosane
message: "I dunno..."

name: Tomosane
message: "Either way, earlier, we were talking about cutting out the nerve fiber bundle that causes 'pain' and keeping it alive in a test tube full of culture fluid."

name: Tomosane
message: "Isolating the nerve fibers, making them fire a signal and then asking 'Where's the pain?' isn't a very scientific question.
It seems to me like you're just finding fault with the results."

name: Yuki
message: "Oh yeah? Well how about this? Saul Kripke said that the fixed indicator--"

name: Tomosane
message: "Oh, save it. I've read books about that kind of stuff and they always end up saying the same things. I've had enough of this."

name: Tomosane
message: "As for the explanation that brain activity is generated by the 'mind,' you can make as many arguments against science as you want from a philosophical standpoint. Most of them would just be ridiculous."

name: Tomosane
message: "There's no point in talking about it."

name: Yuki
message: "Well then, I guess that's it.
Another score for Yuki-nee, the debate master!"

name: Tomosane
message: "What the hell? By your rules, whoever gets the last word in is the winner."

name: Yuki
message: "That's how arguments work on the internets!"

name: Tomosane
message: "So it's how idiots argue?"

name: Yuki
message: "What was that!? You jerk! Apologize to all the home defenders, self-proclaimed novelists, self-proclaimed researchers, self-proclaimed writers, and eroge writers that spew delusions on their so-called 'meditation accounts!' All of whom have too much time on their hands and surf the net late at night debating people they don't even know!"

name: Tomosane
message: "Those guys all sound pretty pathetic..."

name: Yuki
message: "Yeah, you're right. I feel the same about those guys..."

name: Tomosane
message: "Do you...?"

name: Yuki
message: "It's like, 'Get a job!!' right?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Well, I guess so..."

name: Yuki
message: "I mean, you know... Getting back on topic... I'm of the same opinion as you, Tomosane."

name: Yuki
message: "It's not really a fair question to ask 'Where's the pain?' when it comes to nerve fibers in a test tube."

name: Yuki
message: "There's no point in bringing philosophical arguments into scientific attempts to objectify the phenomenon of 'consciousness.'"

name: Yuki
message: "I understand the apprehension to the opinion that 'Science can explain everything!', though."

name: Yuki
message: "I'm not trying to claim that science can 'endlessly expand the range of explanations,' but I'm not sure how you can politely and preemptively argue that something can't be explained by science."

name: Yuki
message: "There's that word 'qualia,' right? It basically means 'sensory qualities,' but the word 'qualia' itself has gotten pretty famous for being so interesting."

name: Yuki
message: "It means the unique 'senses' and 'feelings' that you experience."

name: Yuki
message: "For example, in scientific terms, visible light can be defined in numbers by the wavelength of electromagnetic waves. But the colors that we see aren't these numbers, but things like 'red' and 'blue.' These are 'qualia.'"

name: Yuki
message: "Essentially, by their nature, you can only know what they are internally, through 'sense' and 'feel'. That is what makes them qualia by nature."

name: Yuki
message: "But in truth, qualia are commonly explained in educational TV programming, and most people can understand what they are after seeing examples while watching these shows."

name: Yuki
message: "If it's something that everyone can understand, it's probably a concept that can be objectified in a naturalistic way."

name: Yuki
message: "To me, the moment this was universally accepted as the 'Qualia problem,' it ceased being a philosophical matter and became a scientific question of how to induce 'Qualia'."

name: Tomosane
message: "That reminds me of the idea of 'philosophical zombies,' who often come up in relation to the qualia problem, and are also easily understood by most people."

name: Yuki
message: "The 'philosophical zombie' suggests that other people may not have qualia, but by phrasing the problem that way in the first place, you're assuming that people other than yourself *do* have qualia, or else they wouldn't understand it."

name: Yuki
message: "They're saying that 'the term“Qualia”has become universal.'
That feels like a declaration that 'Consciousness and the mind are now going to be explored in earnest, not by philosophy but by science!'"

name: Yuki
message: "The so-called 'hard problem' of consciousness, no matter how 'hard,' is still nothing more than a 'problem' with a definitive form."

name: Yuki
message: "I read an interview with an A.I. scholar who said that since he was a kid he'd always wondered how to confirm that the color 'red' that he sees is really the same 'red' that others see, but to me, that's exactly the same level as what neuroscience targets."

name: Yuki
message: "There's a lot of hypotheses about 'consciousness' that are currently being scientifically tested. I'm not saying philosophical skepticism isn't valid, but that the scientific side only operates within the scope of its procedures."

name: Yuki
message: "That said, I think philosophers telling scientists 'It's a waste of time trying to unravel the secrets of consciousness' is a dogmatic argument with no basis."

name: Yuki
message: "The scientific view and philosophical view are two completely different things in the first place. Talking about them like they're coming from the same viewpoint only causes confusion, right?"

name: Tomosane
message: "But isn't science already a separate thing from philosophy?"

name: Yuki
message: "In ancient Greece, philosophers believed in three fields of study: physics, ethics, and logic."

name: Yuki
message: "One historically significant book on Newtonian mechanics is titled 'Mathematical Principles of Natural Philosophy.' As you can tell from the inclusion of 'Philosophy' in the title of this book that became a cornerstone of modern physics, physics was thought of as a form of philosophy in Newton's time."

name: Yuki
message: "What I'm trying to say is that all kinds of study are essentially specialized forms of philosophy. Generally speaking, specialized fields of study have been successful in objectifying all kinds of philosophical problems."

name: Yuki
message: "We create a kind of system for impressions, or maybe ideas, of memories. We include everything we recall as having 'occurred', whether through our internal experiences or sensory functions."

name: Yuki
message: "Each individual thing in such a system is called 'reality' with one's current impressions."

name: Tomosane
message: "What's that about? Are you quoting someone?"

name: Yuki
message: "That's Hume! He wrote it in 'A Treatise of Human Nature.'"

name: Yuki
message: "Doesn't the 'with one's present impressions' part make you think?"

name: Tomosane
message: "About what?"

name: Yuki
message: "Oh, nevermind."

name: Yuki
message: "Perhaps philosophy is the work of putting things into an 'actual' form that fits into understandable generalities and regularities."

name: Tomosane
message: "So, now that qualia have become a generality, does that mean philosophy has finished its work when it comes to consciousness and things like that?"

name: Yuki
message: "I don't agree with that at all.
Well, this is just conjecture, but maybe someday science will be able to explain consciousness. We'll be able to reproduce 'consciousness' in artifical intelligence."

name: Yuki
message: "There's no reason right now to think that human consciousness is the only thing that science can't handle.
People can probably think of everything that can be thought."

name: Yuki
message: "Like, in my case, I don't really get how 'numbers' can exist."

name: Tomosane
message: "You mean, like 1, 2 and 3?"

name: Yuki
message: "Yeah, exactly."

name: Yuki
message: "Like, if I hold up my finger, you see that as 'one,' right?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Well, yeah, that's what holding up 'one' finger usually means."

name: Yuki
message: "In Africa, people see the head of a single elephant and call that 'one.' A '1' in a string of mathematical calculations is also called 'one.' Same with the verbal expression of the word 'one,' or a calculator displaying '1' on its screen."

name: Yuki
message: "These are all completely different objects, but we call all of them 'one.'
From here we can use it for things like 1+1=2, and more advanced calculations that let us enhance our lives and chase the fundamental mysteries of the universe."

name: Yuki
message: "People talk about the discovery of zero as a huge historical revelation, but negative numbers are pretty significant too, right?"

name: Yuki
message: "For example, let's say you lend me \130. I use it to buy a can of juice. The money's gone. Since I started with no money and we can't go any lower than zero, I now effectively have 'minus' \130."

name: Yuki
message: "The idea of debt is essentially rooted in negative numbers. This is a pretty odd concept too!"

name: Yuki
message: "Numbers were invented when humanity began farming. The concept of negative numbers would definitely be necessary for managing crop distribution and buying and selling produce."

name: Yuki
message: "From here, math was broadly divided into algebra, geometry, and analysis, gradually growing more complex and uncovering all kinds of laws that affect every conceivable aspect of our world."

name: Yuki
message: "The way I see it, even though we don't really know what 'one' is, or where it originates from, we still use it to 'understand' a myriad of things."

name: Yuki
message: "This is such a crazy mystery! It's so puzzling, it keeps me awake even during my afternoon naps!"

name: Tomosane
message: "Yeah right. You sleep in the middle of the day all the time!"

name: Yuki
message: "Good sleep is essential for a child's development!"

name: Tomosane
message: "What the hell do you mean by 'development'...?"

name: Yuki
message: "Maybe my boobs? It does feel like they've gotten a bit bigger lately..."

name: Tomosane
message: "No way. You can't grow anymore."

name: Yuki
message: "Not so! You can't deny the possibility that they might still grow to reflect your innermost desires."

name: Yuki
message: "If you wish really hard, they might turn into J-cup super tits!"

name: Tomosane
message: "What's that, like the cup size of the J-League?"

name: Yuki
message: "It's your innermost desire!"

name: Tomosane
message: "No it isn't..."

name: Yuki
message: "On top of J-cups, you could even make me grow a penis!
I could use it to stimulate your prosate!"

name: Tomosane
message: "I definitely do not want that. Even if my only alternative was death, that's absolutely never happening."

name: Yuki
message: "I wonder...? As you tried to resist, I'd penetrate you.
As you screamed out 'Aah!!' you'd have a moment of realization. You'd awaken to your reverse-anal desires..."

name: Yuki
message: "'Aah, this is so wrong, it's so wrong, but... why? Why does it feel so good? I never knew my asshole could feel this way... Aah, this is amazing, this incredible feeling in my asshole... I'm losing iiiittt!!'"

name: Yuki
message: "It'd go something like that! It'd be amazing!"

name: Tomosane
message: "No it wouldn't!"

name: Yuki
message: "It'd feel great in the ass!
You'd get hooked on it I bet. I'd stick it in and you'd be all 'Ah, ah～!'"

name: Tomosane
message: "Cut it out already..."

name: Yuki
message: "I'm serious, it would feel incredible! Let's try it!"

name: Tomosane
message: "Absolutely not!
How do you even know how it feels to take it in the ass in the first place?"

name: Tomosane
message: "What's with the sudden silence!?"

name: Yuki
message: "...But, suppose the relation between neurological and mathematical information becomes clearer."

name: Tomosane
message: "What, we're back on this topic again?"

name: Yuki
message: "At any rate, it's been discovered that information produced by the brain, an organ that operates on physical laws, is in a similar format to numerical information anyway, and this reveals the inevitable outcome of the brain producing numbers... Well, seems like a satisfactory explanation to me."

name: Tomosane
message: "How can you go straight back to this after all the anal talk?"

name: Yuki
message: "Shut up and listen! Do you even understand what I've been talking about?"

name: Tomosane
message: "What you've been talking about...

Why we consider so many things as the number 'one,' why we expanded that to the idea of numbers as a whole, and how they can lead us to the mysteries of the universe? And how mysterious all of that is?"

name: Yuki
message: "So you have been paying attention."

name: Yuki
message: "A certain physicist has hypothesized that everything in the universe can be thought of as 'it from bit.' The idea is that every phenomenon, subatomic particles, gravity, nuclear force, everything can be made from two kinds of bits of information, 0 or 1."

name: Yuki
message: "This is more of a hypothesis, or borderline delusion, but if it could be proven on an observable level, my question of 'How can numbers explain the world?' would probably be solved."

name: Yuki
message: "Even if it weren't something that grandiose, if a formal similarity could be found between the brain and the number '1,' the mystery would be solved."

name: Tomosane
message: "But if the 'it from bit' theory were proven, wouldn't all philosophical problems be solved? Not just consciousness, but at a universal level?"

name: Yuki
message: "You think so? I completely disagree.
Even if we objectify the thing we call 'consciousness,' that thing is still different from 'me.'"

name: Yuki
message: "In simple terms, it doesn't matter how much you objectify and generalize things."

name: Yuki
message: "I am me.
Now is the present.
This place is here."

name: Yuki
message: "We'll never be able to truly grasp the fundamental mysteries of 'I, now, and here.'"

name: Yuki
message: "I believe there's a fundamental difference between this question and the so-called 'hard problem.'"

name: Yuki
message: "Even if consciousness could be explained, a lot of 'open' philosophical problems would still remain... If anything, we'd still be close to the fundamental object problem, which couldn't be objectified with the name 'consciousness.'

name: Yuki
message: "Why is there even a word for qualia in the first place?"

name: Yuki
message: "The word 'qualia' is used to objectively describe sensations that are extremely subjective depending on the person, so what use is an objective term like 'qualia?'"

name: Yuki
message: "Even that word 'subjective' I just used doesn't describe how personalized they are, yet 'qualia'is used in a generalized sense and incorporated into philosophical discussions."

name: Yuki
message: "'Subjectivity' would suggest that 'We don't have some“consciousness meter,”' but using 'we' when talking about 'subjectivity' seems strange, right?"

name: Yuki
message: "In fact, there seems to be a lot of confusion in that area.
Wittgenstein, genius that he was, expressed that 'solipsism cannot be said, but it shows itself.' I think that's exactly it."

name: Yuki
message: "It's fine when it's something that's been completely scientifically objectified, but it gets confusing when you add in things that aren't.
Especially when it comes to things like philosophical discussions, it's easy to start incorporating scientific words, but that can complicate things so much that you can't tell what's what."

name: Yuki
message: "Philosophical arguments should only be made from philosophical viewpoints.
It's dangerous to mix science-specific viewpoints in, as it can add confusion to the base of your argument."

name: Yuki
message: "I was reading a book on philosophy recently."

name: Yuki
message: "It said something like 'my body is the world.'
It went on to explain, 'Even in terms of material relationships like those between oxygen and carbon dioxide, my body is circulating and connected on a global scale.'"

name: Yuki
message: "I thought, 'What the hell is up with that?'"

name: Tomosane
message: "Well, isn't that kind of true?"

name: Yuki
message: "No, that stuff has nothing to do with philosophy, does it? That's just the kind of scientific worldview that even a slightly intelligent grade schooler could come up with."

name: Yuki
message: "How should I put it? It's like Fukuoka supremacists who say 'Fukuoka's the best! Huh? Why would I ever leave? There's no better city on Earth!' They never leave Fukuoka for their whole lives, so would they say 'My limit is the limit of Fukuoka!'?"

name: Yuki
message: "Would they say 'My body is unevenly distributed within Fukuoka in the circulation of oxygen, carbon dioxide, protein, calcium, etc. and the boundary-line between my body and Fukuoka is vague, so my limits are the limits of Fukuoka.'?"

name: Tomosane
message: "What's this about Fukuoka all of a sudden?"

name: Yuki
message: "I dunno, just an example."

name: Tomosane
message: "Well, the cyclicity of objects is global in the grand scheme of things, isn't it?"

name: Yuki
message: "That doesn't really matter in this case. If you wanted, you could make a simluation of circulating matter, and watch my body spread over billions of years, and nod your head saying 'My body is evenly distributed around here.' couldn't you?"

name: Yuki
message: "That's still not philosophy, though."

name: Tomosane
message: "Ah, I see, that's not real philosophy.
I get what you're saying. Another incredible insight from the brilliant mind of Yuki-san!"

name: Yuki
message: "What's the big idea, Tomosane? Are you making fun of me?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Of course I am. I hate phony crap like that."

name: Tomosane
message: "Self-important jerk-offs that say stuff like 'Oh that's not *real* such-and-such, this is what *real* such-and-such looks like!' are fucking annoying."

name: Yuki
message: "I know, right!?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Do you!? You're the one that just said 'That's not philosophy!'"

name: Yuki
message: "Yes, I did. What about it?"

name: Tomosane
message: "What's with the backtalk!?
I just said people that say stuff like 'This is what real philosophy is' are full of shit, didn't I?"

name: Yuki
message: "I agree! People that get all nitpicky like 'That's not real philosophy...' are so annoying!"

name: Tomosane
message: "But that's you..."

name: Yuki
message: "But I mean, talking about what's 'real philosophy' is kind of cool, don't you think?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Is it? I find it pretty lame."

name: Yuki
message: "I know, right!?"

name: Tomosane
message: "What the hell do you mean, you know what I mean!?"

name: Yuki
message: "I thought the same! Reading that book, I thought 'God, this is so lame! What is this“transcendental”crap? Stuff like“There's a huge gap between transcendental perception and psychological perception,”that's such a load of bull!'"

name: Tomosane
message: "Then why were you reading a philosophy book in the first place?"

name: Yuki
message: "I guess 'cuz it made me feel cool?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Didn't you just say it was lame!?"

name: Yuki

If 'chuunibyou' means 'second-year middle-schooler sickness,' then just maybe... that's... my... true... form..."

name: Tomosane
message: "Quit trying to look cool with the dramatic pauses..."

name: Yuki

name: Tomosane

name: Yuki

name: Tomosane
message: "It was designed so that office workers and public servants without scientific backgrounds could program for it, so it's no wonder it ended up so long."

name: Tomosane
message: "That said, this is a half-century old programming language, used in legacy systems for stuff like government offices and bank accounting systems. It's used in a lot more infrastructure than you'd think."

name: Yuki
message: "It's not using it that I have a problem with, it's how people in the past were so haphazard with it! Like, there's thousands of lines but the template only allows for comments at the very top! That's how programmers back then got paid more than our generation does, right?"

name: Yuki
message: "Incomprehensibly deep nests, endless strings of 'if' statements... the pain of following the programming logic."

name: Yuki
message: "Even more puzzling, the 'end if' statement that's supposed to be at the end of the program simply doesn't exist. And yet the program mysteriously still functions."

name: Tomosane
message: "Even without 'end if,' there might still be a period. It doesn't give any errors, at least."

name: Yuki
message: "No! There might not be any errors, but it's still processing the code. It shouldn't be able to do that, but somehow it does! And this is the legacy system that major banks use?! I seriously don't get it! It's scary!"

name: Tomosane
message: "I guess so... Well, more than the language itself, I've seen a lot of sloppy source coding."

name: Yuki
message: "Anyhow, isn't this the kind of job guys in their forties and fifties do? Why are you doing a job like this at your age?"

name: Tomosane
message: "It was brought to me by someone in sales, remember?"

name: Yuki
message: "Seems like you've always been stuck taking lame jobs, Tomosane."

name: Tomosane
message: "Well, I'm fine with it. The sales person brought it to be because there's good money to be made from banking computing systems."

name: Yuki
message: "Maintenance is so boring, though..."

name: Tomosane
message: "I'm not really looking for fun in my job, anyway."
While we were having this conversation in my head, I heard a voice call out.

name: Female Employee
message: "Ah, Mamiya-san, do you have a moment?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Sure. What's up?"
I noticed a woman who had joined our company earlier this year was standing at my desk.

name: Female Employee

name: Tomosane
message: "Oh, this?
Well..."
I started looking over the data sheets the new employee had handed to me.

name: Tomosane
message: "Well, there's nothing fundamentally wrong with it.
But this section here that's just a bunch of numbers is kind of hard to make sense of. It might be easier to undersand if you used a graph instead. Can you get me the data for this?"

name: Female Employee
message: "Sure, just a moment."

name: Tomosane
message: "For example, if we do something like this..."
I typed the values from the document into the spreadsheet software and generated a few different types of graphs.

name: Tomosane
message: "Overall, using both a bar graph and a line graph is the best way to illustrate both the actual numbers and the rate of change in a way that's easy to understand."

name: Tomosane
message: "I think making them red and blue would probably be best, but... What do you think?"

name: Female Employee

Jeez, I've already been working here for two months, please try to remember it!"

name: Tomosane
message: "Ah, sorry.
So, Megumi-san, which color scheme do you think works best?"

name: Watanabe

name: Tomosane
message: "Well then, I'll put this data in the network folder."

name: Watanabe
message: "Thank you so much for all your help, Mamiya-san."

name: Tomosane
message: "It was a piece of cake, really. Don't worry about it."

name: Yuki
message: "Didn't seem so simple to me..."

name: Tomosane
message: "What?"

name: Watanabe
message: "Eh?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Oh, sorry. Could you please update it with this revised data? There's a few places where I changed the text a bit, but as long as the sales supervisor doesn't notice, it'll probably get through without any problems."

name: Tomosane
message: "If they have any complaints about it, you can tell them it was all my fault."

name: Watanabe

name: Tomosane
message: "Well, just some stuff about the sales and job sites. Different little things.
You'll understand before long, so just keep this stuff in mind until then."

name: Watanabe

name: Tomosane

would you please deliver this to the sales supervisor?"

name: Watanabe
message: "Yes, sir. Thank you again. Please, let me return the favor sometime."

name: Tomosane
message: "Really, don't worry about it. Anyhow, you'd better try to get that data finished up before lunch."

name: Watanabe
message: "Oh, right. But..."

name: Tomosane

name: Watanabe

Well, thank you for all your help."

name: Tomosane

The new hire bowed her head and went back to her work station.

name: Yuki

name: Tomosane

name: Yuki
message: "To think the same Tomosane who used to be as sharp as a knife's edge could explain something to the lady from another department with such kindness... Your Yuki-nee was shocked!"

name: Tomosane

name: Yuki

name: Tomosane

name: Yuki

name: Tomosane

name: Yuki
message: "Now that you mention it, weren't you both hired at the same time?
I'll bet the only reason he got promoted first is because of his university degree."

name: Tomosane
message: "Since Ito-san was made supervisor his workload's gotten a lot bigger. I'm sure he's under a lot of mental pressure."

name: Yuki
message: "Even if that's true, isn't what you just did basically supposed to be part of his increased workload?"

name: Tomosane
message: "Oh, give it a rest. Businessmen have to deal with all sorts of stuff."

name: Yuki
message: "All sorts, huh..."
Well, it's not that I didn't agree with what Yuki was saying, but that's just where I stood at this job.
Ito-san wasn't very good at explaining things to people, and he was especially bad at dealing with women.
That's why I decided to overlook that data just then.
We always have all kinds of confusing work pushed on us from sales.
As the 'stopper' we try to check the documents we present to the client as much as possible on site, but this can lead to liability problems later on.
Even at that moment, along with the revisions in the graph, I was typing away, rewriting some of the descriptions.
If we sent out that clunky data set as it was, we'd be screwed.
Text that seems to allow for an unlimited number of changes. Text that you're unsure will be presentable, even up to the last minute before the deadline.
That was pretty much the entirety of my work.
The people in sales usually didn't notice, but when they did, I'd get yelled at.
And then, of course, I'd have to explain myself.
I'd talk myself out of it with a calm, polite explanation.
If you can make them say something along the lines of "based on what you said, it seems the responsibility lies solely with So-and-So-san," then they usually leave you alone.
There's a similar story like this.
A long time ago, it seems they'd deliver air mail even during big storms.
They didn't care about the high rate of accidents, the company still kept sending out planes regardless.
So eventualy, the aviators introduced a stipulation.
The new rule stated that "the person who gives the order must ride along with the aviator, should he be asked to."
Basically, if the pilot felt like his life was being put in danger, he was able to make the person giving him his orders get on the plane with him.
This lead to a drastic decline in orders for departures during storms.
The relationship between sales and the job sites is kind of like this too.
Even if the job site goes up in flames, the sales people keep pushing impossible projects onto us because they don't actually feel the pain.
It'd be better if they were wrapped up in the burning wreckage as well.
For people that can't easily imagine the pain of others, the simplest way to get them to be nicer would be to have them experience the same pain.
Most people don't really care about the pain of others, its their own pain that they fear the most.
I've gotten to know the weakness of those kinds of people very well.
That's why even though they're our sales partners, it's easy to control them.
As for the people at the job sites, it's too much of a pain to fight with the sales people, so most of them tend to avoid it, but I take the initiative.
Taking work I can actually get done rather than a bunch of senseless projects is ultimately better for the company.
That mass suicide case.
The senior director and CEO are the only ones that knew I was the one behind that incident.
I'm grateful to the senior director, but I'm also thankful I was able to be hired even though they knew about my past.
Because of that, I basically ended up taking on all the jobs that nobody else in the department wanted to do.
It's not a very big company, so there's a lot of points of contact between the various departments, and lots of friction between them too.
In the middle of all this, all I can do is smile and answer, "Yes sir, yes sir."
It seems this has earned me the nickname "Mamiya-san the Buddha" in the development department.
From Yuki's perspective, maybe I look like the unreasonable one, taking on all this work without complaints.
I'm assigned boring maintenance projects, and then I distract myself by checking data from the sale's department's celebrated "latest system".
Well, I guess I'm just an all-around handyman.

They can hear a pin drop from hundreds of meters away...

name: Yuki
